title: DNA and RNA Stabilization
author: Neilier Junior
license_note: Confirmed via CrossRef DOI metadata: dx.doi.org/10.17504/protocols.io.be2pjgdn
materials: Do not freeze samples in stabilization solution immediately.

steps.title: 935 mL of autoclaved MilliQ water...
steps.instruction: 935 mL of autoclaved MilliQ water 700 g ammonium sulfate Stir until dissolved Add 25 mL of 1 M sodium citrate Add 40 mL of 0.5 M EDTA Adjust to pH 5.2 using concentrated H 2 SO 4 Complete the volume to 1.5 L with autoclaved MilliQ water Store at room temperature. If any precipitation of stabilization solution is seen: heat to 37 °C and agitate to redissolve it.

steps.title: Do not freeze tissues before immersion...
steps.instruction: Do not freeze tissues before immersion in DNA and RNA stabilization solution. Use stabilization solution with fresh tissue only.

steps.title: Add the stabilization solution in an appropriate 1
steps.instruction: Add the stabilization solution in an appropriate 1.5 mL microtube. Use the minimum volume ratio 1:5 (tissue : solution).

steps.title: Cut large tissue samples to approx
steps.instruction: Cut large tissue samples to approx. 3 mm in any single dimension and transfer to microtube containing the solution.

steps.title: Store at 4 °C overnight to...
steps.instruction: Store at 4 °C overnight to allow the solution to thoroughly penetrate the tissue.

steps.title: The DNase and RNase inactivation is reversible
steps.instruction: The DNase and RNase inactivation is reversible. So, do not rinse stabilization solution from samples before DNA and/or RNA extraction.

steps.title: Tissue homogenization should be rapid once...
steps.instruction: Tissue homogenization should be rapid once the tissue is in lysis/denaturation solution to nucleic acids extraction.

steps.title: Remove the stabilization solution from the...
steps.instruction: Remove the stabilization solution from the cells prior to extraction for isolating DNA and RNA from cells. With the aid of a pipette, remove excess ofstabilization solution from the tube and then use an absorbent lab wipe or paper towel to help remove as much of the stabilization solution as possible.
